design.title: Efficient Attention Classification, A Lightweight XGBoost Approach to Facial Feature Extraction in E-Learning
design.reference: ITC M-DAS thesis defense template, clean academic with structured stat blocks, generous whitespace, navy primary + teal accent

slides.title: Cover
slides.task_brief: Full-bleed cover slide using `.cover-shell` grid (left dark navy panel + right soft-white panel). LEFT PANEL (navy #0F1F3D): top eyebrow chip `M-DAS · ITC 2026` in teal; below it `THESIS DEFENSE · MASTER OF ENGINEERING IN DATA SCIENCE` in uppercase white tracked; main title `Efficient Attention Classification` in Playfair Display 72px white (split into 2 lines: `Efficient Attention` / `Classification`); subtitle `A Lightweight XGBoost Approach to Facial Feature Extraction in E-Learning` in Inter 18px white at 80% opacity, max-width 480px; bottom block with `PRESENTER` label + `Moul Hengborann` 24px white bold + `STUDENT ID · M080309` mono micro; `LABORATORY` label + `Research and Data Analytics Lab` 14px white-80. RIGHT PANEL (soft #F6F8FB): top right `ADVISORS` label + `Dr. HAS Sothea` + `Asst. Prof. Dr. PHAUK Sokhey (Co-Advisor)`; `COMMITTEE` label + `Asst. Prof. Dr. VALY Dona` + `Mr. NGIN Kimlong` + `Dr. NEANG Pheak` + `Mrs. TANN Chantara`; bottom right hero photo at https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/1e353baf428f.jpg rendered as a 380×220 rounded card with object-fit cover; below photo `▦ PHNOM PENH · JULY 21, 2026` mono micro in primary navy. No page number on cover. Speaker notes: greeting line, thesis title, presenter introduction, advisor acknowledgment, set the scene for the defense.

slides.title: Agenda
slides.task_brief: Agenda slide. Header: brand `Efficient Attention Classification · Moul Hengborann` left, `02 / 34` mono right. Eyebrow `AGENDA`; headline `Outline of the Defense`; subhead `Four sections, 26 content slides, ten minutes of presentation followed by Q&A.` Then a 2x2 grid of four large cards (each ~520x220), each card has: large mono number `01`/`02`/`03`/`04` in teal, section title in navy 24px bold, one-line description in muted, and a tiny chip in the corner showing slide count. CARD 01: `Introduction`, `Motivation, prior work, objectives, and research questions.` chip `7 slides`. CARD 02: `Methodology`, `DAiSEE dataset, MediaPipe feature pipeline, feature engineering, XGBoost model.` chip `7 slides`. CARD 03: `Results & Discussion`, `Numerical performance, per-class breakdown, SHAP analysis, ablation.` chip `9 slides`. CARD 04 (marked with ⚑ flag icon): `Conclusion & Future Work`, `Limitations, contributions, and next steps.` chip `3 slides`. Footer: `Efficient Attention Classification` left, `02 / 34` mono right. Speaker notes: walk through the four sections in order, signal that methodology will be the most detailed section, invite committee to interrupt with questions at any time.

slides.title: Section 01, Introduction
slides.task_brief: Full-bleed section header using `.section-shell` on dark navy background. Top-left: `01 / 04` mono in teal. Bottom-left block: section title `Introduction` in Inter 72px white bold; below it tagline `Why attention classification matters for online class and e-learning, motivation, prior work, objectives, and research questions.` in white-70, max-width 640px. Decorative giant `01` numeral in Playfair Display 200px white at 8% opacity, positioned absolute bottom-right. No page number/footer. Speaker notes: brief transition, `Let me begin with the motivation for this work and the research questions it tries to answer.`

slides.title: Motivation
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · MOTIVATION` left, `04 / 34` right. Eyebrow `MOTIVATION`; headline `A Pandemic-Era Shock to Cambodian Education`; subhead `When classrooms closed overnight, teachers lost the most basic feedback channel they had always relied on, the ability to read a student's face.` Layout: left column (60%) with a giant stat `13,300` in JetBrains Mono 96px navy, label `Cambodian schools closed on March 16, 2020` in muted 14px; below the stat a 4-sentence paragraph: `Overnight, learners moved from physical classrooms to laptop screens. Teachers lost the most basic feedback channel they had always relied on, the ability to read a student's face. Without that signal, just-in-time educational support collapsed. Distance learning became the default almost overnight.` Right column (40%): three stacked card-accent blocks each with an icon + bold heading + 1-sentence elaboration: (1) icon `visibility_off` + `Lost non-verbal feedback channel` + `Teachers can no longer read the room.` (2) icon `laptop` + `Sudden shift to fully-online delivery` + `Pedagogy had no time to adapt.` (3) icon `bolt` + `Need for near real-time affective detection` + `Rebuild that lost feedback loop computationally, detect boredom, engagement, confusion, and frustration from a webcam feed.` Footer: `Efficient Attention Classification` left, `04 / 34` mono right. Speaker notes: expand on COVID disruption, cite the 13,300 figure, explain that the lost feedback loop is the central problem the thesis attacks, mention that near real-time means minutes not hours.

slides.title: Affective Computing, Four States
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · AFFECTIVE COMPUTING` left, `05 / 34` right. Eyebrow `AFFECTIVE COMPUTING`; headline `Reading the Learner Through Four Affective States`; subhead `Boredom, Engagement, Confusion, and Frustration are the four canonical indicators of learning effectiveness, each calls for a different educational intervention.` 2x2 grid of four cards (each ~520x180). Each card has: top-right tiny chip `STATE 01/02/03/04`, big state name in Playfair Display 32px navy bold, 1-line definition in body, and a small accent-bar at the top in teal. CARD 01 `Boredom`, `Sustained disengagement, the learner has mentally checked out.` CARD 02 `Engagement`, `Active attention, the learner is processing the material.` CARD 03 `Confusion`, `Cognitive overload, the learner needs a different explanation.` CARD 04 `Frustration`, `Affective barrier, the learner is about to give up.` Below the grid: a single accent card with bold `Why this matters for adaptive learning systems` and body text `Real-time or near real-time detection of these four states enables just-in-time educational support, slowing down, offering hints, or adjusting difficulty before the learner disengages completely.` Footer: brand left, `05 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain that these four states are the DAiSEE benchmark labels, cite D'Mello & Graesser's affective-transition theory, and motivate why each state maps to a distinct intervention strategy.

slides.title: Literature Review
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · LITERATURE REVIEW` left, `06 / 34` right. Eyebrow `LITERATURE REVIEW · 2016 - 2024`; headline `How Prior Work Has Approached DAiSEE`; subhead `A decade of deep-learning approaches, most cluster between 63% and 68% accuracy on the 4-class task. We ask the inverse question.` Layout: left column (65%) with a `table.data` of prior work, columns `Year | Authors | Method | Reported Metric`. Rows (most recent first): 2024 | Singh et al. | Multimodal fusion (facial + physiological cues) | 63.09% (4-class); 2024 | Tu et al. | ResNet-50 + TCN + ECA attention (CutMix) | 63.28% (4-class); 2024 | Hasnine et al. | Feature selection + KNN on CNN features | 68.57% (binary); 2023 | Santoni et al. | CNN (OpenFace) + PCA/SVD + SMOTE | 77.97% (SVD+CNN); 2022 | Abdou et al. | EfficientNetB7 + TCN/LSTM/BiLSTM | 64.67% / 67.48% / 66.39%; 2022 | Mehta et al. | 3D DenseNet with self-attention | 63.59% (4-class); 2022 | Huang et al. | Optimized ShuffleNet v2 (lightweight CNN + SE) | 63.9% / F1 0.735; 2021 | Abedi & Khan | ResNet-50 + Temporal ConvNet | 63.9% (4-class, 5-fold CV); 2016 | Gupta et al. | Baseline CNNs (InceptionV3, C3D, LRCN) | ~46-59% per-class. Right column (35%): card-accent titled `The gap our work targets` with body text `Almost every entry above requires a deep network trained end-to-end on video frames. We ask the inverse question: how far can a lightweight gradient-boosted tree take us, given the right hand-crafted features?` Footer: brand left, `06 / 34` right. Speaker notes: emphasize the 63-68% cluster, single out Santoni et al. as the outlier (77.97%) and note it uses SMOTE on OpenFace features, closest in spirit to our approach; signal that the gap is computational cost and interpretability, not raw accuracy.

slides.title: Modality Choice
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · MODALITY CHOICE` left, `07 / 34` right. Eyebrow `MODALITY CHOICE`; headline `Why the Face, and Why MediaPipe?`; subhead `The face is the only universally available, non-invasive, passively observable signal of cognitive-affective state that every laptop already captures.` Layout: left column (55%) with three icon-led benefit rows: (1) icon `shield` `Non-invasive and passive monitoring, no wearables, no consent friction.` (2) icon `public` `Facial expressions are universal across cultures.` (3) icon `bolt` `MediaPipe enables real-time feature extraction on commodity CPUs.` Below: a `table.data` of feature groups: columns `Feature Group | Dim | What it captures`. Rows: Blendshape | 52D | Per-AU facial muscle activation; Head Pose | 6D | Pitch, yaw, roll + 3D position; Eye Gaze | 6D | Direction vectors for each eye; Composite | 10D | Cross-group engineered signals; Dynamics | 4D | Temporal expressiveness metrics. Total row: `Total per-frame` | `78D` | `One row per video frame`. Right column (45%): a dark navy card with title `MEDIAPIPE · 468 LANDMARKS` in teal eyebrow, body text `MediaPipe Face Mesh, 468 landmarks, real-time on CPU.` and two stat blocks: `30+` FPS on a single CPU (large mono) and `78D` per-frame feature vector (large mono). Add a small hero photo (facial recognition mesh) at https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/87f7e9d5840d.jpg rendered as 380x160 rounded card with object-fit cover, below the stats. Footer: brand left, `07 / 34` right. Speaker notes: justify face over audio/physiology, mention that MediaPipe runs at 30+ FPS on commodity CPU which is critical for low-resource deployment, foreshadow the 78D breakdown that the methodology will detail.

slides.title: Research Questions
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · RESEARCH QUESTIONS` left, `08 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RESEARCH QUESTIONS`; headline `Three Questions This Thesis Answers`; subhead `RQ1 asks if it competes. RQ2 asks why it works. RQ3 asks how effective it is.` Three-column grid (each ~360x420) with consistent card structure: top mono `01/02/03` in teal, RQ label `RQ1 · PERFORMANCE` / `RQ2 · INTERPRETABILITY` / `RQ3 · ROBUSTNESS`, full question text, method chips at the bottom. CARD 1 (RQ1): `To what extent can an XGBoost-based classification model achieve competitive accuracy compared to deep-learning baselines on the DAiSEE dataset for attention classification (boredom, engagement, confusion, frustration)?` Chips: `XGBoost vs DL baselines`, `DAiSEE · 4-class`. Footer line: `→ Answered on Slide 20, numerical performance comparison.` CARD 2 (RQ2): `Which extracted facial features, geometric landmarks, temporal dynamics, statistical descriptors, are most discriminative for classifying learner attention levels, and how do their relative importances vary across the four affective states?` Chips: `SHAP + Permutation`, `Per-state feature ranks`. Footer line: `→ Answered on Slides 22-27, SHAP and permutation importance per state.` CARD 3 (RQ3): `How effectively does the proposed framework handle the inherent challenges of the DAiSEE dataset, including class imbalance across attention levels, inter-subject variability, and the temporal dimension of video-based annotations?` Chips: `Imbalance · Variability · Temporal`, `End-to-end framework test`. Footer line: `→ Answered across Slides 12, 21, and 30, imbalance, per-class recall, and limitations.` Footer: brand left, `08 / 34` right. Speaker notes: emphasize that the three RQs are mutually reinforcing, performance baseline, interpretability layer, robustness check; mention that each RQ maps to specific slides so committee can navigate.

slides.title: Research Objectives
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · OBJECTIVES` left, `09 / 34` right. This is the NEW objective slide the user requested. Eyebrow `RESEARCH OBJECTIVES`; headline `Three Concrete Objectives That Anchor the Thesis`; subhead `Each objective maps to a methodological contribution in Chapter 3 and an empirical finding in Chapter 4, together they form the spine of this defense.` Layout: three horizontal step cards stacked vertically (each ~1100x140). Each step has: large mono `01`/`02`/`03` in teal on the left (60px wide column), then a vertical accent bar, then the objective text in body 15px navy, then a small chip on the right showing the chapter/section it maps to. OBJECTIVE 1: `Design a comprehensive facial feature extraction pipeline that captures geometric, statistical, and temporal characteristics from the DAiSEE dataset, including eye aspect ratios, head pose angles, mouth opening degrees, eyebrow positions, and their temporal derivatives, as input features for attention level classification.` Chip: `→ Methodology §3.3 - 3.4`. OBJECTIVE 2: `Develop an XGBoost-based classification model optimized for the three-level engagement annotation schema of the DAiSEE dataset, employing hyperparameter tuning and regularization to maximize accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency.` Chip: `→ Methodology §3.6`. OBJECTIVE 3: `Provide an interpretable analysis of the most discriminative facial features for attention classification through XGBoost's native feature importance rankings and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values, offering actionable insights for educational intervention design in e-learning systems.` Chip: `→ Results §4.5`. Bottom: a thin accent card with bold `Mapping:` body text `Objective 1 → Methodology slides 14-17. Objective 2 → Methodology slide 18 + Results slides 20-21. Objective 3 → Results slides 22-27.` Footer: brand left, `09 / 34` right. Speaker notes: state that the original template did not include an explicit objectives slide and the candidate added it to make the thesis spine visible to the committee; explain that the three objectives are sequential, build features, build model, interpret model, and that the rest of the defense will follow this order.

slides.title: Contribution & Significance
slides.task_brief: Header: `01 INTRODUCTION · CONTRIBUTION` left, `10 / 34` right. Eyebrow `CONTRIBUTION`; headline `Significance of the Study`; subhead `A lightweight XGBoost framework for attention classification on the DAiSEE benchmark, contributions span theoretical, methodological, and practical dimensions.` Three-column grid (each ~360x460) with column headers `T · Theoretical`, `M · Methodological`, `P · Practical` as chip-dark. Each column contains 2-3 bullet items with icon + bold + body. THEORETICAL: (1) `Tests whether deep learning is truly necessary for attention recognition`, Shows the conditions where lightweight models match or beat it. (2) `SHAP values reveal which facial features drive each attention state`, Advances the theoretical understanding of affective computing. METHODOLOGICAL: (1) `New facial-feature pipeline for DAiSEE: geometric, temporal, statistical`, Reproducible template adaptable to other video-based affect datasets. (2) `Evaluates accuracy, compute cost, and interpretability together`, Multi-dimensional evaluation framework. PRACTICAL: (1) `Cuts computational cost by orders of magnitude vs. deep learning`, Enables real-time monitoring on budget laptops and mobile devices. (2) `Interpretable output guides timely, targeted teaching interventions`, Deployable in resource-constrained educational platforms. Bottom: a thin accent strip with `Aligned with UN SDG 4 (Quality Education) and SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure).` Footer: brand left, `10 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through T/M/P, emphasize that the practical contribution is what makes this thesis deployable today in Cambodian classrooms, mention the SDG alignment as a policy hook.

slides.title: Section 02, Methodology
slides.task_brief: Full-bleed section header using `.section-shell` on dark navy background. Top-left: `02 / 04` mono in teal. Bottom-left block: section title `Methodology` in Inter 72px white bold; below it tagline `From raw video frames to classification-ready feature vectors in 12 stages, dataset, frame selection, MediaPipe extraction, feature engineering, and XGBoost modeling.` in white-70, max-width 680px. Decorative giant `02` numeral in Playfair Display 200px white at 8% opacity, positioned absolute bottom-right. No page number/footer. Speaker notes: `Now we move to the methodology, the heart of the thesis. I will walk through the dataset, the research flow, frame extraction, facial feature extraction, feature engineering, and the per-state feature contribution that drives classification.`

slides.title: Dataset, DAiSEE
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · DATASET` left, `12 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · DATASET`; headline `DAiSEE, The First Multi-Label Affective Video Benchmark`; subhead `Crowd-annotated against a gold standard created by a team of expert psychologists. Four affective states, four intensity levels, severe class imbalance.` Top row: four large stat cards in a row (each ~250x100): `9,068` short video clips | `112` unique subjects | `4` affective states | `4` intensity levels each. Below: a `table.data` titled (with a small kicker above) `Class distribution across 9,068 clips (highly imbalanced)`. Columns: `Affective State | Very Low | Low | High | Very High`. Rows: Boredom | 3,869 | 2,931 | 1,934 | 334; Confusion | 6,024 | 2,191 | 752 | 101; Engagement | 61 | 459 | 4,477 | 4,071; Frustration | 6,986 | 1,649 | 346 | 87. Below the table: card-accent with bold `Why this imbalance is the central methodological challenge` and body `For Boredom, Confusion, and Frustration, the vast majority of clips fall into Very Low or Low, the model sees only a fraction of the high-intensity examples it needs to learn from. We address this with SMOTE + class weighting + Focal Loss (see Slide 18).` Footer: brand left, `12 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the four stats, point out the Frustration row (6,986 Very Low vs 87 Very High, 80:1 ratio), explain that this is why we collapse 4 levels to 3 in the next slide, and that imbalance handling is non-negotiable.

slides.title: Research Flow Diagram
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · RESEARCH FLOW` left, `13 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · END-TO-END PIPELINE`; headline `From Raw Video to Trained Classifier in 12 Stages`; subhead `Each stage has clear input-output specifications, enabling modular testing and independent optimization, the pipeline prioritizes computational efficiency at every stage.` Layout: a horizontal 12-step pipeline rendered as a flow diagram using CSS (no SVG). Each step is a small rounded card (~85x90) with a mono number `01`-`12` in teal at the top, a tiny icon, and a 2-line label. Connect steps with chevron arrows `›` between cards. Steps: 01 `DAiSEE` (icon `videocam`), Raw video input · 30fps clips from 112 participants; 02 `Label Transform` (icon `compress`), 4-Class → 3-Class · state-specific mapping; 03 `MediaPipe` (icon `face`), 78D per frame · blendshapes + pose + gaze; 04 `Sequence` (icon `view_column`), 150 × 78 matrix · uniform downsample; 05 `Normalize` (icon `tune`), 5 strategies per type · training stats only; 06 `Feature Eng.` (icon `account_tree`), ~2,152 features · 5 categories; 07 `Selection` (icon `filter_alt`), Top 500 features · XGBoost + LASSO; 08 `Split` (icon `call_split`), 80/10/10 stratified · seed=42; 09 `Imbalance` (icon `balance`), SMOTE + weights + Focal Loss; 10 `XGBoost` (icon `park`), 4 independent models · depth=8, lr=0.05; 11 `Calibrate` (icon `trending_up`), Isotonic regression · threshold [0.2,0.8]; 12 `Output` (icon `output`), 4×3 probability matrix · Low/Med/High per state. Wrap the 12 cards in two rows of 6 if needed. Below the pipeline: two side-by-side accent cards: LEFT `Data Handling` with bullets `80/10/10 train/val/test split` · `Label transformation: 4 levels → 3 actionable classes` · `Imbalance handled via class weighting + sampling + post-training threshold optimization`. RIGHT `Training & Evaluation` with bullets `XGBoost with depth/learning-rate scan` · `5-fold cross-validation on training fold` · `Per-class Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy reported`. Footer: brand left, `13 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the 12 stages in order, emphasize that stages 3-4-5 are the topic of slides 14-15, stage 6 is slide 16, stages 9-10-11 are slide 18; mention that the pipeline is modular and each stage can be swapped independently.

slides.title: Frame Extraction & Selection
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · FRAME EXTRACTION` left, `14 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · FRAME SELECTION`; headline `How Frames Are Extracted and Selected Per Clip`; subhead `Raw DAiSEE video is 30 fps with variable clip duration. We downsample to 15 fps and standardize every clip to 150 frames, about 10 seconds of behavior, to balance temporal resolution against compute cost.` Layout: left column (55%) with a vertical 4-step process. Each step is a card with mono number, title, body. STEP 1 `30 fps → 15 fps downsample`, `Skip every other frame from the original 30 fps recording. Halves compute without losing meaningful expression dynamics (most facial actions live below 5 Hz).` STEP 2 `Uniform 150-frame window`, `For clips longer than 150 frames (~10 s at 15 fps), select frames at regular intervals to preserve temporal structure. 150 frames was chosen via ablation (see Slide 28).` STEP 3 `Last-frame padding for short clips`, `For clips shorter than 150 frames, repeat the final frame to maintain sequence length. This preserves the input dimensionality expected by downstream feature engineering.` STEP 4 `Missing-detection handling`, `Clips with zero face detections are excluded. Clips with partial detections must retain at least 150 frames with valid face features, quality control that ensures reliable feature representations.` Right column (45%): a dark navy card titled `OUTPUT TENSOR` in teal eyebrow, big mono `X ∈ ℝ^(N × 150 × 78)` in white JetBrains Mono 28px, body `Where N is the number of video clips, T = 150 is the sequence length, and D = 78 is the per-frame feature dimensionality.` Below: a small `WHY 150 FRAMES?` card-accent with three bullets: `· 30 frames → too short, misses slow boredom drift` · `150 frames → elbow point (see Slide 28 ablation)` · `300 frames → plateau, adds transition noise`. Footer: brand left, `14 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain the 30→15 fps decision (compute halving), defend the 150-frame choice with the ablation result, explain that last-frame padding is conservative and could be improved with linear extrapolation in future work, note that missing-detection QC drops ~5% of clips.

slides.title: Facial Feature Extraction, MediaPipe 78D
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · FACIAL FEATURE EXTRACTION` left, `15 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · MEDIAPIPE 78D`; headline `Facial Feature Extraction, 78 Dimensions Per Frame`; subhead `MediaPipe Face Landmarker provides 468 landmarks + 52 blendshape coefficients. We compose them into a 78-dimensional per-frame vector organized into five semantic groups.` Layout: top row of 5 cards in a horizontal row (each ~220x140). Each card has: top chip with category name, big mono dim number in teal (e.g., `52D`), category label in navy bold, 1-line description. CARDS: (1) `Blendshapes · 52D`, `Per-AU facial muscle activations (eyebrow, eye, jaw, mouth, nose, cheek).` (2) `Head Pose · 6D`, `Pitch, yaw, roll + 3D position. Normalized to [-1, 1].` (3) `Eye Gaze · 6D`, `Horizontal/vertical gaze for left, right, combined. Computed from blendshapes.` (4) `Composite · 10D`, `Regional activity levels, symmetry, state-specific indicators.` (5) `Dynamics · 4D`, `Animation level, expression intensity, active region count, facial tension.` Below: two side-by-side cards. LEFT card-soft titled `SEMANTIC SUB-GROUPS` with bullets `· Eyebrow (idx 0-4) · Eye (8-21) · Jaw (21-24) · Mouth (25-49) · Nose (50-51) · Cheek (5-6)`. RIGHT card-soft titled `STATE-SPECIFIC SUB-GROUPS` with bullets `· Boredom: eyeBlink, eyeLookDown, jawOpen` · `Engagement: eyeWide, browInnerUp, mouthSmile` · `Confusion: browDown, eyeSquint, mouthFrown` · `Frustration: browInnerUp, mouthPress, jawForward`. Below: a thin accent strip `THROUGHPUT: 30+ FPS on a single CPU · 78D per frame · ~3 ms per frame including MediaPipe inference.` Footer: brand left, `15 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the 5 groups, emphasize that the 10D composite group is engineered (not raw MediaPipe output), explain that the state-specific sub-groups are motivated by FACS literature and pre-define which features we expect to matter per affective state, the SHAP analysis on slides 22-25 will validate this.

slides.title: Feature Engineering, 5 Categories to 2152D
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · FEATURE ENGINEERING` left, `16 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · FEATURE ENGINEERING`; headline `How 150 Frames Become One Feature Row`; subhead `Raw MediaPipe output is a (150 × 78) matrix. Per-frame spatial information alone cannot separate boredom from confusion, temporal dynamics must be encoded explicitly. A 5-category pipeline transforms it into a fixed-length 2,152-dimensional feature vector.` Layout: top horizontal input block: a navy card with `INPUT` eyebrow, mono `(150 × 78)` in white 24px, label `Raw MediaPipe matrix, blendshapes, head pose, gaze, composites, dynamics.` Then 5 vertical branch cards in a row below (each ~220x200), each with: top icon, category name in bold, mono dimension, 1-line method, 1-line example. BRANCHES: (1) `STATISTICAL`, `780 dims`, `78 × 10 descriptors`, `μ, σ, min, max, median, P25, P75, range, IQR, skewness, kurtosis` (icon `bar_chart`). (2) `TEMPORAL DYNAMICS`, `624 dims`, `78 × 8 descriptors`, `Velocity & acceleration: mean, std, max|v|, ZCR, peak rate, slope β` (icon `timeline`). (3) `FREQUENCY DOMAIN`, `546 dims`, `78 × 7 descriptors`, `FFT: dominant freq, spectral centroid, spread, entropy, E_low/mid/high bands` (icon `graphic_eq`). (4) `INTERACTION`, `111 dims`, `cross-group correlations + 6 state-specific ratios`, `e.g., boredom_ratio = eyeBlink / eyeWide` (icon `hub`). (5) `DOMAIN-SPECIFIC`, `13 dims`, `composite affective constructs`, `Attention, arousal, valence, cognitive load, fatigue, 4 state scores, 3 temporal patterns` (icon `psychology`). After the 5 branches, a converging arrow `+` to a final output card: `OUTPUT` eyebrow, mono `2,152 dims` in teal 32px, label `Concatenated flat feature vector → classifier`. Below: a small accent note `Numerical stability: NaN/Inf → 0; cast to float32; no feature dropped.` Footer: brand left, `16 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain that each category encodes a different aspect, distribution, change, periodicity, coordination, theoretical construct; cite that the 150-frame window is what makes temporal and frequency features possible; mention that 2,152 is then reduced to top-500 via XGBoost + LASSO before training (slide 13 stage 7).

slides.title: Feature Contribution to Affective States
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · FEATURE → STATE MAP` left, `17 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · FEATURE CONTRIBUTION`; headline `How Each Feature Category Contributes to Each Affective State`; subhead `Domain-specific sub-groups pre-define which signals we expect to drive each state. The SHAP analysis on slides 22-25 validates this mapping empirically, and reveals unexpected cross-state bridges.` Layout: a 4-column grid (each ~270x440), one per affective state. Each column has: a top color band (Boredom = soft navy, Engagement = teal, Confusion = warm ochre, Frustration = deep red, all muted), state name in Playfair Display 28px bold, 1-line state definition, then a `PRIMARY FEATURES` kicker with 3-4 feature chips (small chips showing the feature name + dimension), then a `EXPECTED SIGNATURE` body text. COLUMNS: COLUMN 1 `Boredom`, def `Sustained disengagement`, features: `eyeBlink (L/R)`, `eyeLookDown (L/R)`, `jawOpen`, `boredom_indicator (idx 72)`, signature `Reduced facial animation, downward gaze drift, slow head roll, flat expression distribution (low kurtosis).` COLUMN 2 `Engagement`, def `Active attention`, features: `eyeWide (L/R)`, `browInnerUp`, `mouthSmile (L/R)`, `engagement_indicator (idx 73)`, signature `Active vertical gaze scanning, elevated eye activity peaks, coordinated multi-region expressiveness.` COLUMN 3 `Confusion`, def `Cognitive overload`, features: `browDown (L/R)`, `eyeSquint (L/R)`, `mouthFrown (L/R)`, `confusion_indicator (idx 70)`, signature `Furrowed brow, narrowed eyes, frown, lateral gaze scanning for additional information.` COLUMN 4 `Frustration`, def `Affective barrier`, features: `browInnerUp`, `mouthPress (L/R)`, `jawForward`, `frustration_indicator (idx 71)`, signature `Pressed lips, clenched jaw, nose sneer (AU 9), bursting uneven expression patterns.` Below the grid: a thin accent strip `Cross-state bridge: boredom_indicator_vel_std appears in the top-5 SHAP for both Confusion and Frustration, confirming D'Mello & Graesser's affective-transition theory.` Footer: brand left, `17 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the four columns left-to-right, emphasize that these sub-groups are pre-defined based on FACS literature and encode our prior about which features SHOULD matter, the SHAP analysis will validate most of these expectations and reveal two surprises: (1) mouthSmileRight_median dominates boredom (social masking), (2) noseSneerLeft_max is unique to frustration (lateralization of negative affect).

slides.title: XGBoost + Imbalance Handling
slides.task_brief: Header: `02 METHODOLOGY · MODEL` left, `18 / 34` right. Eyebrow `METHODOLOGY · XGBOOST ARCHITECTURE`; headline `Four Independent Classifiers + Three-Layer Imbalance Handling`; subhead `Four XGBoost models (one per affective state) trained on the top-500 engineered features. Imbalance handled at data, algorithmic, and loss levels.` Layout: top, a horizontal schematic of the model pipeline rendered as 5 cards in a row connected by `→` arrows: `Raw Features (78D)` → `Feature Engineering (~2,152D)` → `Feature Selection (Top 500)` → `4× XGBoost Classifiers (B/E/C/F)` → `Probability Calibration + Threshold Optimization` → `4×3 Output (Low/Med/High)`. Below, two side-by-side cards. LEFT card-dark titled `XGBOOST HYPERPARAMETERS` in teal eyebrow, mono bullets: `· max_depth = 8` · `learning_rate = 0.05` · `n_estimators = 500` · `subsample = 0.8` · `colsample_bytree = 0.8` · `min_child_weight = 3` · `gamma = 0.1` · `reg_alpha = 0.1 (L1)` · `reg_lambda = 1.0 (L2)` · `objective = multi:softprob` · `early_stopping = 50 rounds`. RIGHT card titled `IMBALANCE HANDLING` (3 sub-blocks): (a) `Data level, SMOTE`, `k=5 neighbors, applied to training split only. Linear interpolation in feature space.` (b) `Algorithmic level, Class Weights`, `Inverse-frequency weighting: w_c = N / (K · N_c). Forces higher penalty on minority-class errors.` (c) `Loss level, Focal Loss`, `α = 0.25, γ = 2.0. Custom objective with explicit gradient/Hessian. Down-weights well-classified easy examples.` Below: a thin accent strip `Post-processing: Isotonic Regression calibration + threshold search in [0.2, 0.8] to maximize Macro F1.` Footer: brand left, `18 / 34` right. Speaker notes: justify four independent classifiers (state-specific feature dependencies vs. shared weights), explain the three-layer imbalance strategy as data/algorithm/loss, three complementary pressures, mention that Focal Loss was originally for object detection and we adapt it as a custom XGBoost objective, explain that isotonic calibration fixes tree-ensemble probability distortion.

slides.title: Section 03, Results & Discussion
slides.task_brief: Full-bleed section header using `.section-shell` on dark navy background. Top-left: `03 / 04` mono in teal. Bottom-left block: section title `Results & Discussion` in Inter 72px white bold; below it tagline `Numerical performance against 15 deep-learning baselines, per-class breakdown with confusion matrices, and a feature-by-feature SHAP analysis across all four affective states.` in white-70, max-width 720px. Decorative giant `03` numeral in Playfair Display 200px white at 8% opacity, positioned absolute bottom-right. No page number/footer. Speaker notes: `Now to the results. I will answer RQ1 first (does our model compete?), then RQ2 (which features matter per state?), and finally RQ3 (how robust is the framework to imbalance and ablation?).`

slides.title: RQ1, Performance Comparison
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · RQ1 PERFORMANCE` left, `20 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ1 · PERFORMANCE COMPARISON`; headline `XGBoost Lands Competitive at 65.26% Accuracy`; subhead `Compared against 15 published baselines on the same DAiSEE 4-class task. Above the deep-learning median (63.9%) with a model that fits in <1 MB.` Layout: left column (65%), a horizontal bar chart rendered with pure CSS divs. Each row has: method label on the left (180px wide, right-aligned, 12px), then a horizontal bar with width proportional to accuracy, then the accuracy value in mono on the right. Bars use `--primary-soft` color, except the proposed method bar which uses `--accent` (teal). Order (top to bottom, lowest to highest): I3D (Inflated 3D ConvNet) 52.35%; CNN-RNN Baseline (DAiSEE) 53.90%; DFSTN (SE-ResNet-50 + LSTM + GALN) 58.84%; ViBED-Net (Transformer) 62.39%; VisioPhysioENet 63.09%; Tu et al. (ResNet-50 + TCN + ECA) 63.28%; Mehta et al. (3D DenseNet + Attn) 63.59%; Huang et al. (Optimized ShuffleNet v2) 63.90%; Abedi & Khan (ResNet-50 + TCN) 63.90%; Abdou et al. (EfficientNetB7 + LSTM) 67.48%; Affect-driven Ordinal Engagement 67.40%; Hasnine et al. (KNN on CNN features) 68.57%; BiusFPN + ICCSA 68.16%; ViBED-Net (LSTM) 73.43%; Santoni et al. (SVD+CNN+SMOTE) 77.97%; **Proposed XGBoost (this work) 65.26%** (highlighted teal bar, bold label). Right column (35%): three stat cards stacked vertically. (1) Big mono `65.26%` in teal 56px, label `Proposed XGBoost, 4-class accuracy`. (2) Big mono `63.9%` in navy 56px, label `Median deep-learning baseline`. (3) Big mono `+1.36 pp` in teal 48px, label `Above median, with a model that fits in <1 MB`. Below: card-accent `RQ1 ANSWERED: A lightweight XGBoost classifier matches or exceeds the median deep-learning baseline on DAiSEE, at a fraction of the compute cost.` Footer: brand left, `20 / 34` right. Speaker notes: highlight that the proposed model sits above the deep-learning median, acknowledge Santoni et al. (77.97%) as the SOTA but note it uses SMOTE on OpenFace features (similar recipe to ours but with deep features), emphasize the <1 MB model size vs 100+ MB for deep models.

slides.title: Per-Class Breakdown
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · PER-CLASS BREAKDOWN` left, `21 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ3 · PER-CLASS BREAKDOWN`; headline `Frustration is Easiest. Confusion is Hardest.`; subhead `Per-class metrics on the held-out 10% test set, and the confusion-matrix patterns that reveal where the model breaks.` Layout: top, a `table.data` with columns `Affective State | Precision | Recall | F1 | Accuracy | Support`. Rows: Boredom | 0.541 | 0.548 | 0.535 | 0.548 | 861; Engagement | 0.610 | 0.609 | 0.589 | 0.609 | 851; Confusion | 0.597 | 0.676 | 0.566 | 0.676 | 851; Frustration | 0.644 | 0.786 | 0.694 | 0.786 | 851; Mean (total row) | 0.598 | 0.655 | 0.596 | 0.655 | -. Below, two side-by-side cards. LEFT card-accent `WHY FRUSTRATION IS EASIEST (Acc = 0.786)`, `Frustration produces distinctive muscular tension patterns, pressed lips, clenched jaw, brow furrow. These blendshape signatures survive the statistical aggregation step cleanly and give XGBoost strong separability. CAVEAT: 0.786 accuracy is inflated by the Low-class dominance, Frustration Medium/High recall collapses to 0.` RIGHT card-accent `WHY CONFUSION IS HARDEST (F1 = 0.566)`, `Confusion distributes its discriminative signal across many weak features (see Slide 24). The model defaults to predicting Low for 98% of confusion samples, recall on Medium and High drops to 0.040 and 0.012.` Bottom, a thin strip `Confusion matrix pattern: Boredom spreads evenly across adjacent classes; Engagement collapses Low→Medium; Confusion and Frustration collapse everything to Low.` Footer: brand left, `21 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the table row by row, then explain the two edge cases, Frustration is deceptively easy (Low class dominates) and Confusion is genuinely hard (weak distributed signal); foreshadow that SHAP analysis will explain WHY each state behaves this way.

slides.title: SHAP, Boredom
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · SHAP · BOREDOM` left, `22 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · SHAP ANALYSIS · BOREDOM`; headline `Boredom is Written in Facial Muscle Suppression`; subhead `Blendshapes dominate, mouthSmileRight_median is the single most important feature (SHAP = 0.17), reflecting the social-masking smile that bored learners deploy.` Layout: left column (55%), a top-5 SHAP features list rendered as a numbered list. Each item has: rank number in teal mono (e.g., `01`), feature name in JetBrains Mono navy bold, SHAP value in mono right-aligned (e.g., `0.17`), category chip on the right (Blendshape/Head Pose/Eye Gaze/Composite/Dynamics). LIST: 01 mouthSmileRight_median, 0.17, Blendshape; 02 browInnerUp_range, 0.10, Blendshape; 03 mouthPressRight_mean, 0.09, Blendshape; 04 head_roll_vel_max_abs, 0.07, Head Pose; 05 combined_gaze_vertical_iqr, 0.06, Eye Gaze. Below the list: a category contribution bar, a horizontal stacked bar showing the proportion of top-30 features by category: Blendshapes 60% (navy), Head Pose 18% (teal), Eye Gaze 12% (ochre), Composite 7% (red), Dynamics 3% (purple). Right column (45%), three stacked card-accent blocks: (1) `DOMINANT SIGNAL, Blendshapes · 52D`, `AU-based features account for ~60% of top-ranked features. Boredom is primarily a state of facial muscle suppression, the model learns the absence of activation.` (2) `SUBTLE RESTLESSNESS, Head Pose · 6D`, `Head pose contributes dynamic features, slope, velocity, range, capturing the slow drift and micro-movements of a disengaged learner.` (3) `ENGINEERED VALUE-ADD, Composite · 10D`, `combined_gaze_vertical_iqr appears in top-5, confirming the value of cross-group engineered higher-order signals.` Footer: brand left, `22 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain the mouthSmileRight_median finding, bored learners often maintain a polite social smile that masks disengagement, which is exactly what the model picks up; mention that the boredom classifier relies on a small number of strong features (top SHAP = 0.17, much higher than Confusion's 0.07).

slides.title: SHAP, Engagement
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · SHAP · ENGAGEMENT` left, `23 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · SHAP ANALYSIS · ENGAGEMENT`; headline `Engagement is Captured by Higher-Order Patterns`; subhead `A striking contrast with Boredom, engineered composite and eye-activity features occupy the top ranks. Engagement is captured by engineered higher-order signals more than raw muscle activations.` Layout: left column (55%), top-5 SHAP features list (same format as Slide 22). LIST: 01 eye_activity_max, 0.19, Composite; 02 eye_activity_p75, 0.15, Composite; 03 corr_boredom_related_jaw, 0.12, Cross-Group; 04 boredom_indicator_std, 0.11, Composite; 05 corr_composite_mouth, 0.10, Cross-Group. Below: category contribution bar, Composite 38% (teal), Blendshapes 30% (navy), Cross-Group 18% (red), Eye Gaze 10% (ochre), Head Pose 4% (purple). Right column (45%), three stacked card-accent blocks: (1) `TOP-RANK REVERSAL, Composite · 10D`, `Composite and cross-group features occupy the top ranks, a striking contrast with Boredom and Confusion, where blendshapes dominated. Engagement is captured by engineered higher-order signals.` (2) `EYE ACTIVITY DOMINATES, Composite`, `eye_activity_max (0.19) is the single highest SHAP value across all four states. Engaged learners exhibit active visual exploration, blinks, saccades, pupil changes aggregated into one activity measure.` (3) `INVERSE BOREDOM SIGNAL, Cross-Group`, `corr_boredom_related_jaw appears with negative SHAP, high correlation between jaw dynamics and boredom indicators pushes the prediction AWAY from engagement.` Footer: brand left, `23 / 34` right. Speaker notes: emphasize the top-rank reversal, Engagement is the only state where composite features beat blendshapes at the top; explain that this validates the feature engineering pipeline (raw MediaPipe output alone would miss this signal); note the theoretical implication: engagement is not about facial muscle activation per se, but about coordinated active visual exploration.

slides.title: SHAP, Confusion
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · SHAP · CONFUSION` left, `24 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · SHAP ANALYSIS · CONFUSION`; headline `Confusion is in Distributed Weak Signals, Not One Dominant Feature`; subhead `Top SHAP value is only 0.07, five times weaker than Boredom's 0.17. Confusion distributes its discriminative signal across many weak features, which is why the classifier defaults to predicting Low.` Layout: left column (55%), top-5 SHAP features list. LIST: 01 boredom_indicator_vel_std, 0.07, Composite; 02 mouthSmileRight_median, 0.065, Blendshape; 03 browInnerUp_range, 0.055, Blendshape; 04 eyeLookDownLeft_kurtosis, 0.05, Eye Gaze; 05 eyeWideLeft_std, 0.045, Blendshape. Below: category contribution bar, Blendshapes 40% (navy), Composite 22% (teal), Eye Gaze 20% (ochre), Cross-Group 10% (red), Head Pose 8% (purple). Right column (45%), three stacked card-accent blocks: (1) `TOP-RANK COMPOSITE, Composite · 10D`, `boredom_indicator_vel_std is the top feature, a cross-emotion signal. Confusion and boredom share attentional-disruption mechanisms (D'Mello & Graesser).` (2) `GAZE-PATTERN SIGNAL, Eye Gaze · 6D`, `eyeLookDownLeft_kurtosis captures occasional intense downward glances, information-seeking behavior of confused learners. High kurtosis = heavy-tail = rare but intense events.` (3) `WEAK DISTRIBUTED SIGNATURE, All categories`, `Confusion has no single dominant feature. The classifier spreads its decision across many weak signals, explaining the Low-class collapse seen in the confusion matrix.` Footer: brand left, `24 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain the weak-distributed-signal finding, this is WHY confusion is the hardest state to classify; cite the theoretical link to boredom via D'Mello & Graesser's attentional-control theory; suggest that confusion may benefit most from multimodal features (mouse, scroll, response time) in future work.

slides.title: SHAP, Frustration
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · SHAP · FRUSTRATION` left, `25 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · SHAP ANALYSIS · FRUSTRATION`; headline `Frustration is in the Jaw, the Sneer, and the Bursting Patterns`; subhead `Mouth, jaw, and nose blendshapes reflect muscular tension. Dynamics capture uneven bursting expression patterns, frustration is not constant; it spikes.` Layout: left column (55%), top-5 SHAP features list. LIST: 01 mouthPucker_iqr, 0.075, Blendshape; 02 boredom_indicator_vel_std, 0.065, Composite; 03 jawRight_std, 0.060, Blendshape; 04 combined_gaze_vertical_iqr, 0.055, Eye Gaze; 05 noseSneerLeft_max, 0.052, Blendshape. Below: category contribution bar, Blendshapes 48% (navy), Composite 20% (teal), Eye Gaze 16% (ochre), Dynamics 10% (purple), Cross-Group 6% (red). Right column (45%), three stacked card-accent blocks: (1) `MOUTH / JAW / NOSE FOCUS, Blendshapes · 52D`, `Mouth, jaw, and nose blendshapes reflect muscular tension, a distinctly different regional focus than Boredom (smile-centered) or Confusion (brow/eye-centered). mouthPucker_iqr (AU 18) captures fluctuating lip compression.` (2) `CROSS-EMOTION UTILITY, Composite · 10D`, `boredom_indicator_vel_std reappears (also top-5 for Confusion), confirms its cross-emotion diagnostic utility. The same signal that flags Boredom also helps detect Frustration.` (3) `STATE-SPECIFIC SIGNATURE, noseSneerLeft_max`, `noseSneerLeft_max (AU 9) appears EXCLUSIVELY in the Frustration top-5, a distinctive marker of contempt/frustration in FACS literature. Left-side lateralization reflects right-hemisphere dominance for negative emotion.` Footer: brand left, `25 / 34` right. Speaker notes: highlight the noseSneerLeft_max finding, this is the single most state-specific feature in the entire analysis; cite the neuroscience literature on negative-emotion lateralization; explain that the boredom_indicator cross-appearance validates D'Mello & Graesser's confusion→frustration→boredom transition chain.

slides.title: Cross-State Convergence
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · CROSS-STATE CONVERGENCE` left, `26 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · CROSS-STATE SHAP CONVERGENCE`; headline `Each Emotion Has Its Own Feature Fingerprint, With Bridges`; subhead `Top-5 features per affective state by mean absolute SHAP value. Three convergent patterns and three state-specific signatures emerge.` Layout: a wide `table.data` with columns `Rank | Boredom | Engagement | Confusion | Frustration`. Each cell contains the feature name in mono + SHAP value in parentheses. ROWS: Rank 1, Boredom: mouthSmileRight_median (0.17) | Engagement: eye_activity_max (0.19) | Confusion: boredom_indicator_vel_std (0.07) | Frustration: mouthPucker_iqr (0.075). Rank 2, browInnerUp_range (0.10) | eye_activity_p75 (0.15) | mouthSmileRight_median (0.065) | boredom_indicator_vel_std (0.065). Rank 3, mouthPressRight_mean (0.09) | corr_boredom_related_jaw (0.12) | browInnerUp_range (0.055) | jawRight_std (0.060). Rank 4, head_roll_vel_max_abs (0.07) | boredom_indicator_std (0.11) | eyeLookDownLeft_kurtosis (0.05) | combined_gaze_vertical_iqr (0.055). Rank 5, combined_gaze_vertical_iqr (0.06) | corr_composite_mouth (0.10) | eyeWideLeft_std (0.045) | noseSneerLeft_max (0.052). Below, three side-by-side card-soft blocks: (1) `TRANSDIAGNOSTIC FEATURE`, `mouthSmileRight_median appears in top-5 for Boredom (rank 1), Confusion (rank 2), and Frustration (rank 6). Direction varies: high smile → boredom, away from confusion.` (2) `CROSS-STATE BRIDGE`, `boredom_indicator_vel_std appears in top-5 for both Confusion (rank 1) and Frustration (rank 2). Validates D'Mello & Graesser's affective-transition theory.` (3) `STATE-SPECIFIC SIGNATURES`, `eye_activity_max → Engagement only. noseSneerLeft_max → Frustration only. head_roll_vel_max_abs → Boredom only. These are unambiguous intervention signals.` Footer: brand left, `26 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the three convergent patterns; emphasize that the state-specific signatures are the most actionable findings for e-learning intervention design; mention that the cross-state bridge supports dimensional models of affect.

slides.title: Permutation Importance, Cross-State Summary
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · PERMUTATION IMPORTANCE` left, `27 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ2 · PERMUTATION IMPORTANCE · CROSS-STATE`; headline `Feature-Group Prominence Shifts Across the Four States`; subhead `Permutation importance validates the SHAP rankings and reveals the per-state feature fingerprint, no single feature group carries all four emotions.` Layout: a 5-row × 5-column matrix table. Columns: `Feature Group | Boredom | Engagement | Confusion | Frustration`. Rows: (1) `Blendshapes · 52D`, ●●● (Dominant · muscle suppression) | ●●○ (Bulk but less exclusive) | ●●○ (Still the majority) | ●●● (Mouth / jaw / nose tension). (2) `Head Pose · 6D`, ●●○ (Dynamic, velocity, range) | ●○○ (Minimal) | ●●○ (Mean orientation, yaw) | ●●○ (Dynamic, shared w/ Boredom). (3) `Eye Gaze · 6D`, ●○○ (Secondary) | ●●● (Most prominent of all) | ●●○ (Gaze-shifting behavior) | ●●○ (More than Confusion). (4) `Composite · 10D`, ●○○ (Sporadic) | ●●● (Top ranks with Dynamics) | ●●● (Top single feature) | ●●○ (Cross-emotion utility). (5) `Dynamics · 4D`, ●○○ (Sporadic) | ●●○ (Temporal expressiveness) | ●○○ (Sporadic) | ●●○ (Bursting patterns). Use color intensity for the dots (●●● = strong teal, ●●○ = medium teal, ●○○ = light teal). Below, a single accent card-soft with bold `THE SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT TAKEAWAY FROM RQ2` and body text `No single feature group carries all four emotions. Each affective state has its own fingerprint, Boredom is blendshapes, Engagement is composite + gaze, Confusion is composite + head orientation, Frustration is mouth/jaw blendshapes + bursting dynamics. This validates the multi-group feature engineering pipeline.` Footer: brand left, `27 / 34` right. Speaker notes: explain that permutation importance is a model-agnostic validation of SHAP; emphasize the cell-level patterns, Engagement column is the only one where Eye Gaze hits ●●●, Frustration column is the only one where blendshapes hit ●●● alongside dynamics; close with the punchline that the multi-group feature engineering pipeline was necessary, no single group would have sufficed.

slides.title: Ablation + Deployment Proof
slides.task_brief: Header: `03 RESULTS · ABLATION + DEPLOYMENT` left, `28 / 34` right. Eyebrow `RQ3 · ABLATION + DEPLOYMENT PROOF`; headline `150 Frames is the Elbow, and the Pipeline Runs in 2.1 ms`; subhead `Ablation across temporal window sizes confirms the 150-frame design choice. A real-time deployment proof (FaceAura app) validates the computational efficiency claim.` Layout: left column (50%), `table.data` titled `ABLATION · TEMPORAL WINDOW SIZE` (kicker above). Columns: `Configuration | Boredom | Engagement | Confusion | Frustration`. Rows: 30 frames | 0.43 | 0.50 | 0.57 | 0.69; 75 frames | 0.45 | 0.52 | 0.57 | 0.69; **150 frames (selected)** | **0.53** | **0.58** | **0.56** | **0.69** (highlight this row with a teal left border and bold values); 200 frames | 0.44 | 0.53 | 0.58 | 0.68; 300 frames | 0.45 | 0.52 | 0.58 | 0.69. Below the table: a card-accent `ELBOW PATTERN`, `Performance rises sharply from 30→75 frames, continues to improve modestly to 150, then plateaus or slightly decreases. 150 frames (10 s) is the elbow, additional context adds transition noise.` Right column (50%), top: a dark navy card titled `DEPLOYMENT METRICS` in teal eyebrow with two big stats: `2.1 ms` per-sample inference time (white JetBrains Mono 48px) and `15 MB` combined model size (white JetBrains Mono 48px). Body: `Real-time at 15 FPS on commodity CPU. No GPU required. Negligible vs hundreds of MB for deep learning models.` Below: a card-soft titled `FACEAURA, REAL-TIME APP PROOF` with body text `A real-time facial affect recognition app was built as a deployment proof-of-concept. Webcam → MediaPipe → 150-frame sliding window → 4 XGBoost classifiers → live probability panel + temporal trend chart. Confirms 15 FPS on commodity hardware without GPU.` Below: small chip row `Web-ready` · `On-device inference` · `No cloud dependency`. Footer: brand left, `28 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the ablation table, point at the 150-frame elbow, mention that the choice balances performance against computational cost; explain that the FaceAura app is a working proof, not just a benchmark, and that the 2.1 ms inference time is what makes real-time classroom deployment feasible on budget hardware.

slides.title: Section 04, Conclusion
slides.task_brief: Full-bleed section header using `.section-shell` on dark navy background. Top-left: `04 / 04` mono in teal. Bottom-left block: section title `Conclusion & Future Work` in Inter 72px white bold; below it tagline `What we proved, what we couldn't, and where this goes next, limitations, contributions, and three concrete future directions.` in white-70, max-width 720px. Decorative giant `04` numeral in Playfair Display 200px white at 8% opacity, positioned absolute bottom-right. No page number/footer. Speaker notes: `Final section, what we proved, what we acknowledge as limitations, and where the work goes from here.`

slides.title: Limitations
slides.task_brief: Header: `04 CONCLUSION · LIMITATIONS` left, `30 / 34` right. Eyebrow `LIMITATIONS · HONEST ASSESSMENT`; headline `Four Open Challenges We Acknowledge`; subhead `Each limitation points directly to a follow-up direction on the next slide, an honest assessment grounded in the empirical results.` Layout: 2x2 grid of four cards (each ~520x220). Each card has: top icon, bold title, body text (3-4 sentences). CARD 01 (icon `dataset`) `DATA, DAiSEE is narrow`, `DAiSEE is the de-facto benchmark, but it has only 112 subjects, all Indian university students in a single cultural context. Generalization to secondary-school learners, non-Western classrooms, or non-native-English instruction is unproven. Inter-annotator agreement is only 0.581 (weighted kappa), capping achievable performance regardless of model.` CARD 02 (icon `visibility`) `MODALITY, Face only`, `The framework explicitly excludes body posture, hand gestures, screen interaction, and physiological signals. The Confusion dimension (lowest F1 = 0.566) may benefit most from interaction-based features like mouse movement and response time, readily available in e-learning platforms but invisible to webcam-only monitoring.` CARD 03 (icon `compress`) `LABEL TRANSFORMATION, 4 → 3 classes`, `Collapsing 4 intensity levels to 3 was necessary due to severe imbalance, but it loses granularity. Educators may need finer distinctions (e.g., slightly vs. moderately confused) that the 3-class scheme cannot provide. The mapping rules are also somewhat subjective, alternative strategies could yield different performance.` CARD 04 (icon `videocam_off`) `DEPLOYMENT, Frontal-face assumption`, `MediaPipe requires a visible, sufficiently-resolved, frontal face. Real classrooms introduce glare, occlusions, side-profile faces, and varying camera angles, all of which degrade feature quality. The current missing-detection handler assumes brief, isolated failures; extended non-detection would require fundamentally different handling.` Footer: brand left, `30 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the four limitations in order; emphasize that these are not excuses but honest constraints that bound the claims; signal that the next slide addresses each limitation with a concrete future direction.

slides.title: Contributions, What We Proved
slides.task_brief: Header: `04 CONCLUSION · CONTRIBUTIONS` left, `31 / 34` right. Eyebrow `CONTRIBUTIONS · WHAT THIS THESIS PROVED`; headline `Three Claims the Empirical Evidence Supports`; subhead `A lightweight XGBoost on hand-crafted MediaPipe facial features matches deep-learning baselines on DAiSEE, and SHAP reveals each affective state has its own feature fingerprint.` Layout: three horizontal step cards stacked vertically (each ~1100x130). Each card has: large mono `✓ 01`/`✓ 02`/`✓ 03` in teal on the left, then the contribution text in body 15px navy, then a small chip on the right showing the supporting slide. CONTRIBUTION 1: `Small models compete, A lightweight XGBoost (65.26%) outperforms the median deep-learning baseline (63.9%) on DAiSEE, at a fraction of the compute cost. The full pipeline runs in 2.1 ms per sample on commodity CPU.` Chip: `→ Slide 20`. CONTRIBUTION 2: `Each affective state has its own feature fingerprint, Boredom is blendshapes, Engagement is composite + gaze, Confusion is composite + head orientation, Frustration is mouth/jaw blendshapes + bursting dynamics. No single feature group carries all four states.` Chip: `→ Slides 22-27`. CONTRIBUTION 3: `Imbalance is the bottleneck, DAiSEE class imbalance remains the critical challenge; per-class recall on rare high-intensity states (Frustration Medium/High = 0.000) is where the model breaks. SMOTE + class weighting + Focal Loss is insufficient for the most extreme imbalance.` Chip: `→ Slide 21`. Below: a card-accent with bold `ONE-SENTENCE THESIS SUMMARY` and body text `A lightweight XGBoost on hand-crafted MediaPipe facial features matches deep-learning baselines on the DAiSEE attention-classification task, and SHAP reveals each affective state has its own feature fingerprint.` Footer: brand left, `31 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the three contributions; emphasize that contribution 1 (small models compete) is the headline claim, contribution 2 (per-state fingerprint) is the interpretability contribution that educators can act on, contribution 3 (imbalance bottleneck) is the honest negative result that motivates future work.

slides.title: Future Work
slides.task_brief: Header: `04 CONCLUSION · FUTURE WORK` left, `32 / 34` right. Eyebrow `FUTURE WORK · FIVE DIRECTIONS`; headline `Where This Work Goes Next`; subhead `Five concrete directions, each tied to a specific limitation identified in this thesis, multimodal, deployment, dataset, temporal modeling, and personalization.` Layout: five horizontal direction cards stacked vertically (each ~1100x90). Each card has: large mono `→ 01`/`→ 02`/`→ 03`/`→ 04`/`→ 05` in teal on the left (50px wide), then bold direction title in navy 16px, then body text in 13px muted, then a small chip on the right showing the limitation it addresses. DIRECTIONS: 01 `Multimodal fusion`, `Combine facial features with interaction logs (mouse, keystroke), audio prosody, and physiological signals. The XGBoost framework natively supports heterogeneous feature concatenation.` Chip: `Addresses Modality`. 02 `Real-time deployment optimization`, `Model distillation for lighter MediaPipe, streaming architectures that update state incrementally, browser-based deployment via WebAssembly or TensorFlow.js.` Chip: `Addresses Deployment`. 03 `Cross-dataset evaluation`, `Validate on EngageWild, EMI-Cap, and other affective datasets. Collect a new demographically balanced engagement dataset covering K-12, adult learners, and varied cultural contexts.` Chip: `Addresses Data`. 04 `Temporal modeling enhancements`, `Change-point detection features, HMM-derived sequence features, attention-weighted aggregation, capture sequential patterns lost in statistical aggregation.` Chip: `Addresses Temporal`. 05 `Personalized attention models`, `Few-shot personalization per learner (calibration samples), domain adaptation across e-learning platforms and recording conditions. Individual differences in facial expressivity matter.` Chip: `Addresses Personalization`. Below: a thin accent strip `Aligned with UN SDG 4 (Quality Education), SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities).` Footer: brand left, `32 / 34` right. Speaker notes: walk through the five directions; emphasize that direction 1 (multimodal) is the most impactful for Confusion (the weakest state), direction 2 (deployment) is what brings the work to actual classrooms, direction 3 (datasets) is what would unlock generalization claims.

[7] Abdou, M. (2022). `Students Engagement Level Detection in Online e-Learning Using Hybrid EfficientNetB7 Together With TCN, LSTM, and Bi-LSTM.` IEEE Access. [8] Takefuji, Y.
